ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general plant-based milk analog class; no specific flavoured/sweetened subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 category A0FJP covers plant-based milk substitutes; flavoured variant mapped as closest defensible code. No exact flavoured subcode confirmed in published FoodEx2 catalogue; approximate match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No confirmed SNOMED CT code for flavoured plant-based milk; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: plant base (soy, almond, oat, rice, or coconut)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one glass of approximately 200 ml, providing around 100 kcal and 12.5 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured Plant-Based Milk — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for flavoured plant-based milk (cr_id F01020006). Saturated fat not reported in HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for sweetened soy milk (FDC ID 1097542) as closest approved analogue. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient detail not reported in HPB source; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; sweetened soy milk analogue used as closest approved reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for generic flavoured plant-based milk in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from sweetened soy milk entries (GI range 43–63, midpoint ~55) published in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55); gi_category is therefore set to 'low' per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 15.82) / 100 ≈ 8.7. Estimation basis clearly documented.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (6.25 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage; sodium is moderate at 58 mg/100 g but the sugar load independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should choose unsweetened plant-based milk alternatives (sugar ≤0.5 g/100 g) to avoid the blood-pressure-raising effect of regular added-sugar intake from beverages.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.25 g sugar/100 g and only 0.41 g dietary fibre/100 g; liquid sugar raises postprandial blood glucose rapidly with minimal fibre buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this flavoured plant-based milk and substitute unsweetened variants (0 g added sugar) to prevent rapid postprandial glucose spikes associated with sugar-sweetened beverages.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk; fat content is low (2.08 g/100 g) and saturated fat is estimated low (~0.30 g/100 g), which is favourable, but the added sugar load is the primary concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, limit flavoured sweetened plant milks and prefer unsweetened versions; the low saturated fat content is a positive attribute but does not offset the cardiovascular risk from habitual added-sugar beverage consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 6.25 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; absence of meaningful omega-3, polyphenols, or fibre >3 g/100 g means no anti-inflammatory benefit is conferred.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose unsweetened soy or almond milk to obtain plant protein and healthy fats without the pro-inflammatory added-sugar load present in flavoured variants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured plant-based milk is a commercially produced, dairy-free beverage made from a plant base — commonly soy, almond, oat, rice, or coconut — blended with water, added sugars or sweeteners, natural or artificial flavourings, and stabilisers. It is ultra-processed and sold chilled or shelf-stable. The texture is thin and milk-like; the flavour varies by base and added flavouring (e.g., vanilla, chocolate, strawberry). Per 100 g it provides approximately 50 kcal, 0.83 g protein, 2.08 g fat, 7.91 g carbohydrate, and 6.25 g sugar, with 0.41 g dietary fibre and 58 mg sodium. The high sugar content (6.25 g/100 g) classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO dietary guidance. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension due to rapid postprandial glucose elevation and the blood-pressure-raising effect of habitual SSB consumption. Cardiovascular risk is moderate — low saturated fat is favourable but added sugar is a concern. Unsweetened plant-based milk alternatives are strongly preferred for all three conditions. Calcium and iron are reported as 0 mg/100 g in the HPB source, indicating this product is not fortified. It is vegan, dairy-free, and generally halal-suitable, but allergen status depends on the plant base used.
common_user_queries: Is flavoured plant milk good for diabetics?
Can I drink sweetened soy milk if I have hypertension?
How much sugar is in flavoured oat milk?
What is the glycemic index of sweetened plant milk?
Is flavoured almond milk suitable for weight loss?
Does flavoured plant milk contain calcium?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01020006, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central sweetened soy milk as closest approved analogue and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database sweetened soy milk range with estimation basis documented. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source, indicating this product is not calcium- or iron-fortified; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based product containing no animal-derived cholesterol; naturally zero. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this generic flavoured plant-based milk category are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F01020006, and no exact product match exists in USDA FoodData Central to supply these values without unacceptable estimation uncertainty. Saturated fat estimated at 0.30 g/100 g from USDA FoodData Central sweetened soy milk (FDC ID 1097542) as closest approved analogue; this is an approximation and may vary by plant base. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database sweetened soy milk range (43–63); GI 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per standard classification; actual GI will vary by plant base, sugar type, and processing. This record covers the generic flavoured plant-based milk category; specific product variants (soy, almond, oat, coconut) may differ in protein, fat profile, and allergen status. The food_group 'Dairy and eggs' is retained from the input as a classification convention for dairy alternatives in the Singapore HPB taxonomy.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 hard gate requirements.
Iteration 1: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 hard gate requirements.
Iteration 2: Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving to 8.7 based on (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 15.82) / 100 ≈ 8.7.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low'. GI value of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this classification rationale explicitly.
